description: L1-5 use pick-and-place. L6+ uses swap. Structural rules (hierarchy, border=identity, indicator order) transfer across paradigms. Only the interaction mechanic changes.
8 levels teach: flat→hierarchy→multi-group→border-identity→multi-parent→swap-grid→palindrome-nesting→cross-reference. Each level builds capacity required by the next.

sc25 deep analysis. Spell mapping CORRECTED: L-shape=TELEPORT, diamond=SIZE_CHANGE, vertical=FIREBALL. Background sprite (dtfljwuit-4) IS the maze — pixel transparency = passable. Fireball must hit edusagitv switch (not door directly) to remove ALL doors. Player must be same row/col as target. Scale 2=4x4 collision 4px step, scale 1=2x2 collision 2px step. SDK level transitions may not reset rendered position to level definition — need to track actual position. L3 needs: align y with edusagitv(55,22), face RIGHT, fireball, navigate through cleared corridor to exit(22,37). Budget=50 actions per level.
sc25 6/6 COMPLETE. All levels verified. Total baseline=216.

TWO PANELS: left is read-only context, right is editable program. Binary encoding: N arrow toggles per column, value = sum(2^i). Maps to command table: 0=NOP, 1=LEFT, 2=RIGHT, 3=DOWN, 33=UP, 5=ROT90, 8=SCALE+, 9=SCALE-, 10=dRIGHT, 63=COLOR. Click confirm to play right panel program. Block resets after each play — win checked at end.

ANIMATION DISCOVERY: SDK returns multi-frame tensor (N,64,64) for animation steps. All frames in one response. GIF capture + viewer display working. Animation plays once in viewer then swaps to static frame.

WAYPOINT MECHANIC: checkered 4x4 sprites (ccjcukzyce) are docking points. When block lands exactly on one (same scale), the block home position UPDATES. Block still resets after play, but resets to NEW home. This enables multi-play paths around walls that cant be traversed in 6 steps. Key visual: checkered pattern squares on the grid = plan your route through them.

tn36 COMPLETE 7/7. Player-piano programming game. Key discoveries: binary bit encoding for commands, right panel is editable (not left), checkered squares are waypoints for multi-play paths, portals toggle on step%3==2 with inclusive-bound collision — must time passage through kill zone.

tr87 6/6 COMPLETE. 137 actions, baseline 317, 231% efficiency. Rewrite rule puzzle with symbol cycling. L5 alter_rules: edit rules to satisfy fixed answer. L6 tree_translation: A->B->C chain with name matching.

tu93 step mechanics: each env.step(direction) is ONE frame in a 3-phase cycle. Phase 0: player input. Phase 1: player settling + arrow activation. Phase 2: entity settling. SDK returns 8 frames for a complete turn. 1 frame = blocked move. 15 frames = death/game_over. Grid nodes at maze-relative positions divisible by 6. Player sprite has yellow indicator pixel at [0,1] that shifts with rotation.

ARROW ACTIVATION POSITIONS (CORRECTED): rot=0(UP)->player at (ax,ay-6) ABOVE. rot=180(DOWN)->player at (ax,ay+6) BELOW. rot=90(RIGHT)->player at (ax+6,ay) RIGHT. rot=270(LEFT)->player at (ax-6,ay) LEFT. Arrow faces TOWARD the player position. Walking into arrow position from ANY OTHER direction destroys the arrow safely.

BOUNCER TIMING CRITICAL: bouncers are NOT player-independent. Walking into a bouncer position changes its trajectory. Blocked moves do NOT advance entity states. Bouncer timing has even/odd parity that cannot be shifted with blocked-move waste. Must find paths with correct natural parity or destroy blocking entities first.

L6 DEEP ANALYSIS: 6 arrows form interlocking dependency chain. Destroying one exposes player to another's activation zone. Correct destruction order: (30,18)→(18,36)→(12,36)→(18,30)→(12,30)→(12,18). But 2 bouncers patrol paths between destruction targets. Bouncer at (18,6) has period-2 with locked parity (unreachable on even steps from start). Bouncer at (36,30) sweeps bottom row. Arrow-bouncer interaction makes timing extremely complex. NEED PERCEPTUAL APPROACH — play interactively watching bouncer positions frame by frame.

L8 DELAYED ENTITY MECHANICS (verified from source): entity queue starts [180,180] (own facing x2), then player's ACTUAL directions appended. Entity processes one queued direction per turn. Blocked moves (player hitting wall) do NOT append to queue or advance entities. Entity replays player's EXACT directions (not reversed). Entity is 2 turns behind due to initial [180,180]. From (24,6) entity goes DOWN DOWN to (24,18) in 2 turns, then follows player. Only valid moves from (24,18): UP (toward entity), LEFT, RIGHT. Going LEFT feeds entity LEFT direction = entity goes LEFT from (24,18) = AWAY from corridor. But entity arrives at (24,18) on step 8 regardless. Player cannot return to (24,18) at step 8. Need 3+ step detour through L/R before sprinting UP. All paths through x=24 tested — entity always intercepts due to 2-turn lag and corridor structure.

L8 CRITICAL DISCOVERY: blocked moves freeze ALL entity state (no Phase 2 runs). Verified: 3 blocked DOWNs at (24,18) keep entity at (24,6). But first VALID UP still collides — entity settles at (24,12) in Phase 2 of the same turn the player arrives there. Entity processes queue [180,180,player_dirs...]. Two simple paths exist to exit, BOTH go through (24,18) triggering entity. 1000+ L/R waste combos tested. Entity is invincible and follows player directions with 2-turn lag. NEED INTERACTIVE COACHING to find the missing mechanic.

L8 Rule 0 applied: played visually, watched entity move step by step. Confirmed: entity moves DOWN from (24,6) one grid node per valid player move. Blocked moves freeze ALL state. Tried RIGHT from (24,18) to (30,18), then blocked UPs (froze entity), then LEFT — entity catches up in Phase 2. Tried going RIGHT past entity column — (30,18) has NO UP path. Every approach converges to same collision: player and entity meet at (24,12) or (24,18) during Phase 2 settling. Only 2 simple paths to exit exist, BOTH through (24,18). GENUINE KNOWLEDGE GAP — need coaching or fresh perspective.
vc33 COMPLETE 7/7. 184% efficiency. Dual-button puzzle: brown (instant wall swap) + blue (animated goal transfer). Win condition requires structural alignment, not just position.

TWO BUTTON TYPES: brown squares at edges = instant wall movement. Blue rectangles in middle = animated goal transfer (starts inactive, turns olive when ready). Blue buttons are free — animation doesn't consume move budget.

ENABLER PATTERN: when a button stops working (depleted), clicking a different button that seems to do nothing resets it. Enablers can chain 3+ deep. Try all buttons when stuck, not just the ones that visibly move things.

POSITION != WIN: goal at target position does NOT always clear the level. The game checks which wall section the goal is in, not just where it is. Blue button animations fix this structural mismatch. If position is right but level didn't clear, activate a blue button.

DISCOVERY STRATEGY: spend first ~8 clicks mapping what each button does (click once, observe). Brown buttons come in pairs (forward/reverse). Identify which moves the goal toward the target. Then play strategically, not randomly.

LLM must track objects, endpoints, objectives, and predictions EVERY step. Without explicit tracking, intention amnesia causes drift — stating one plan then executing something unrelated on the next move. Format: objects list, current objective, planned action, predicted result, verify after.
meta: General principle applicable to ALL games. The #1 failure mode is losing track of what you're doing mid-sequence.

description: 0.8B model finds all 7 interactive objects but cannot form action sequences to solve levels. The planning gap — not perception — is the binding constraint. Scene description (describe_scene()) adds spatial context but doesn't help when the model can't reason about object relationships or goal states.

All 7 objects produce ~1px changes. With such tiny visual feedback, the model cannot distinguish 'this click moved toward the goal' from 'this click did nothing useful'. Larger visual changes (like lp85's 293px) give stronger learning signal.
